teamObjective.explanation: Player 0 holds only low non-trump clubs and cannot realistically finish the hand. With the partner down to 2 cards, the best team objective is to lead a low single that the partner may be able to beat and convert into a finishing run.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner likely has a small number of cards (2), so they may be close to going out. They probably retain at least one card strong enough to beat a low club single, but this is uncertain given no public plays.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents have 3 cards each and have played nothing. They likely possess higher ranks or trumps that can beat any low single, so a strong lead would still probably be captured. They may also be waiting to suppress a high lead.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of clubs is the least committal play. It avoids burning the best low card, invites the partner to take the lead if they can, and limits the value opponents gain if they overtake.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 6 is our strongest low card, but as a non-trump it is still likely beaten by opponents or by a partner holding a high card. Leading it now wastes our best chance to win a later trick.
Playing the 5 is slightly stronger but still weak; it does not meaningfully improve the chance of retaining the lead and gives up a card that could be useful later.
The 4 is a reasonable middle option, but the 3 is even lower and therefore safer when the goal is to let a partner or future trick develop.

risks: An opponent may beat the 3 with a higher non-trump or any trump and seize the lead.
The partner may be unable to help, leaving player 0 to defend with a very weak hand.
No bombs or trumps are available to recover the lead.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the lowest card, player 0 keeps higher cards for later tricks and gives the partner the best chance to win a cheap lead. If the trick is lost, the loss is minimal.

notes: All beliefs are probabilistic; only the new_game event has occurred, so no hidden cards can be asserted as fact.
